message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients with comorbid conditions have a higher risk of mortality with SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) infection, but the impact on heart failure patients living near a disease hotspot is unknown. Therefore, we sought to characterize the prevalence and outcomes of COVID-19 in a live registry of heart failure patients across an integrated health care system in Connecticut.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this retrospective analysis, the Yale Heart Failure Registry (
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="clintrialgov" xlink:href="NCT04237701">NCT04237701</jats:ext-link>
                    ) that includes 26,703 patients with heart failure across a 6-hospital integrated health care system in Connecticut was queried on April 16
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020 for all patients tested for COVID-19. Sociodemographic and geospatial data as well as, clinical management, respiratory failure, and patient mortality were obtained via the real-time registry. Data on COVID-19 specific care was extracted by retrospective chart review.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19 testing was performed on 900 symptomatic patients, comprising 3.4% of the Yale Heart Failure Registry (N=26,703). Overall, 206 (23%) were COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    . As compared to COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>-</jats:sup>
                    , these patients were more likely to be older, black, have hypertension, coronary artery disease, and were less likely to be on renin angiotensin blockers (P<0.05, all). COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>-</jats:sup>
                    patients tended to be more diffusely spread across the state whereas COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    were largely clustered around urban centers. 20% of COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    patients died, and age was associated with increased risk of death [OR 1.92 95% CI (1.33–2.78); P<0.001]. Among COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    patients who were ≥85 years of age rates of hospitalization were 87%, rates of death 36%, and continuing hospitalization 62% at time of manuscript preparation.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this real-world snapshot of COVID-19 infection among a large cohort of heart failure patients, we found that a small proportion had undergone testing. Patients found to be COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    tended to be black with multiple comorbidities and clustered around lower socioeconomic status communities. Elderly COVID-19
                    <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                    patients were very likely to be admitted to the hospital and experience high rates of mortality.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: COVID-19 Infections and Outcomes in a Live Registry of Heart Failure Patients Across an Integrated Health Care System
